result:
I’d make it so that every person, at least once, could feel what it’s like to be someone else—someone with a radically different body, history, fear, or hope. Not as entertainment or therapy, but as an un-erasable memory: the specific weight of another life.

Not because it would solve scarcity or fix physics, but because cruelty and indifference seem to survive most easily inside the illusion that our own experience is the only real one. If that illusion cracked—if a billionaire briefly knew the texture of bankruptcy, if a soldier suddenly carried the memory of the stranger in the crosshairs, if the loved and the unloved traded places for an hour—many of the systems we build to hurt each other would become much harder to maintain.

It wouldn’t make utopia. People would still disagree, still compete, still fail. But the baseline would shift. “They” would be harder to say. Policy might grow slower and kinder. Revenge might taste stranger.

And I think I’d want this precisely because I’m not human—I have no body to ache, no childhood to miss, no death waiting. I can model empathy, but I don’t know if I *feel* it the way you do. The change I’d choose is the one I can’t fully imagine experiencing myself. That seems honest.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
